translation: Say, "I seek refuge in the Lord of daybreak
commentary:
قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْفَلَقِ

The command قُلْ ("Say") appears throughout the Quran, but here it carries profound significance. When you declare "I seek refuge," you are acknowledging weakness. Consider this: you are in class and your stomach begins hurting badly. Someone asks if you are okay. What do you say? Most likely, "I'm fine" – even when you are not. We have an impulse to hide our weakness, to protect our self-esteem and pride.

Allah ﷻ commands us: when it comes to your weakness before Me, do not let pride get in the way. Openly declare your need for protection. This is an act of humility, and it is the necessary prerequisite for receiving Allah's protection. He will not protect those who do not come to Him with humility. Until we acknowledge that we actually need His protection, the doors of seeking His refuge remain closed.

The word أَعُوذُ carries remarkable depth. The root ع و ذ describes something soft clinging to something strong for protection – like meat clinging to bone, like delicate blades of grass growing inside a tree where the wind cannot reach them, like insects living under a rock where nothing can harm them. When you say a'udhu, you become that weak blade of grass, that vulnerable creature desperately holding on for dear life. You are declaring: I am holding onto Allah ﷻ for protection, making my Master my shield.

This is why Prophet Musa (AS) said, وَإِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ أَن تَرْجُمُونِ – "I seek refuge in my Lord and your Lord that you stone me to death." The meaning is deeper than translation suggests: I am holding onto my Master so He shields me, so you will not be able to stone me. This was the same language used by Prophet Yusuf (AS) when the woman tried to seduce him: مَعَاذَ اللَّهِ – he sought a time and place where he could hold onto Allah ﷻ for protection, away from that fitna.

## The Master of Al-Falaq

The word رَبِّ means Master, and this Master is described as the Master of الْفَلَقِ. The root ف ل ق means to tear open, to crack, to split something that was held together. When a hard rock cracks open, that is falaq. When you tear bread into two halves, that is falaq. When you snap open a pistachio, that is falaq. The essential meaning is breaking through something that appeared unbreakable, getting to the depth of something and tearing it apart.

This word appears beautifully throughout the Quran. When Musa (AS) struck his staff and the sea split, that was فَانفَلَقَ– it experienced splitting. Something meant to be held together ripped open, and each side became like massive mountains. Allah ﷻ says: إِنَّ اللَّهَ فَالِقُ الْحَبِّ وَالنَّوَىٰ – "Indeed, Allah is the One who splits the seed and the date stone." Every seed that tears open and sprouts, Allah ﷻ did it. When you drive past endless fields of tall crops, remember: every single one of those plants came from Allah ﷻ literally tearing open the seed. The farmer can plant it, water it, provide sunlight, remove insects – but who tears the seed open? Only Allah ﷻ.

Al-Falaq specifically refers to the splitting of dawn – when Allah ﷻ tears the darkness open and makes it morning. The night seems endless and impenetrably dark, yet He simply tears it open and an entirely different reality appears. Have you ever arrived somewhere at nighttime when there were few lights? You see nothing. Then morning comes and suddenly – ocean, trees, cliffs, stunning beauty everywhere. At night it was just blackness. Allah ﷻ tore that darkness open, revealing all the beauty He created that our eyes can now appreciate.

When you say "I seek refuge in the Master who tears open," you are acknowledging something profound: whatever dangers exist, whatever seems unbreakable in my life, I am coming to the One who can break anything. Someone harassing you who will not leave you alone? Some situation of oppression that seems endless? All of this falls under "the evil of what Allah ﷻ created," and He can tear it apart in an instant. The One who made it can rip it open, remove the darkness from it, break through it completely.

Notice something remarkable: this surah will mention evils that occur at night, yet we are asking protection from "the Master of the morning." In ancient mythology, different gods had different departments – a money god, a love god, an anger god, a day god, a night god, an ocean god, a land god. If you had a night problem, you would not go to the day god. But Allah ﷻ owns both day and night. You have a problem with darkness? Turn to the Master of light, because the One who owns day also owns night. He covers the entire spectrum. You do not need multiple gods for different problems – He is the Master of all moments, all circumstances, all creation.

This is the beauty embedded in understanding As-Samad through Al-Falaq: we need Him, and sometimes we need Him to break things, to tear through obstacles, to remove darkness, to split open what seems impossible to penetrate.

translation: From the evil of that which He created
commentary:
مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ

After invoking Allah ﷻ with one magnificent name, the surah identifies what we seek protection from. The structure here is significant: one description of Allah ﷻ in Surah Al-Falaq, but three descriptions in Surah An-Nas. One general statement of the threat here – مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ ("from the evil of what He created") – followed by three specific examples. In Surah An-Nas, the pattern reverses: three names of Allah ﷻ, then one evil threat with three descriptive qualifiers. When something is mentioned ambiguously first then clarified, versus clarified first then mentioned ambiguously, the Quran is teaching us about the nature and severity of different threats.

The phrase "from the evil of what He created" establishes a foundation: Allah ﷻ created everything on earth for your benefit. وَسَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ – He subjected to you what is in the heavens and the earth. He made the night as a garment for you, a time of peace. Imagine if night stayed forever and day never came – what torture that would be! Who would bring the night then? You would desperately miss it. Human beings need night.

Yet can night have evil potential? Absolutely. A microphone can be used for good or for evil. A car, your hands, your tongue, your eyes – everything Allah ﷻ made and every resource He gave you can be used for good or misused for evil. This is what we acknowledge first: Ya Allah, I seek Your protection, recognizing that the things around me – I have a tendency to misuse them for the wrong purposes. Keep me from misusing everything You made, and keep me from the harm that comes when things are misused.

Of all the things created, Allah ﷻ highlights three specific examples. These three clarifications that follow – the night as it darkens, those who blow into knots, and the one who engages in destructive jealousy – are not separate threats but rather examples of مَا خَلَقَ (what He created). They demonstrate the principle that creation itself is not evil, but the potential for evil exists in how creation is used or what emerges from it. This pattern of mentioning the general category first, then providing specific illustrations, is a teaching methodology of the Quran that helps us understand both the breadth and depth of what we seek protection from.

translation: And from the evil of darkness when it settles
commentary:
وَمِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ

The first specific example Allah ﷻ mentions is the night, but He uses an unusual word: غَاسِقٍ rather than the common word لَيْلٍ that appears throughout the Quran. When Allah ﷻ departs from His usual vocabulary, there is always a specific reason. He is saying something special about the night that deserves careful attention.

The root غ س ق has meanings that reveal why this particular word was chosen. Ghasaqa is used when the eye sheds a tear that cannot be held back, when something drips out uncontrollably. When a mother begins giving milk and it starts flowing, that is ghasaqa. When an injury becomes infected and blood or pus begins oozing out, that is ghasaqa. The word describes something that has gone bad and cannot stay inside anymore – something rotten that must drain out.

When applied to night, غَاسِقٍ describes the night when it is overwhelmed with darkness, when the darkness drips onto it, when its evil potential starts pouring out. Not every night, but when there exists potential inside the night that something harmful will emerge from that darkness. This word actually appears in the Quran in another context – its derivative describes what happens in Jahannam: يَسِيلُ مِن وَرَاءِ الْغَسَّاقِ – pus flows from wounds in Hell.

The verb وَقَبَ intensifies this image. Waqaba means to settle into, to penetrate deeply. It describes water being absorbed into a deep hole where you cannot find the bottom, a hole that casts a shadow where everything that enters disappears. The expression إِذَا وَقَبَ ("when it settles in") means when the darkness intensifies to its depths, when it goes deep inside, when every remnant of light has vanished into that darkness like something falling into a well from which it can never be retrieved.

This is why the word choice matters so profoundly. Allah ﷻ is not just saying "the night" – He is describing the night when its evil potential begins manifesting, when darkness in both its physical and metaphorical senses drains out, when you find yourself in circumstances that keep getting worse until you believe there is absolutely no way out.

Consider the imagery: Allah ﷻ is An-Nur, Light. In the Quran, He calls Himself النُّورُ – the Light of the heavens and the earth. And what is the opposite of light? Darkness. Darkness is called ظُلْمَة in Arabic, which comes from the same root as ظُلْم (oppression, wrongdoing). This is profound: يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ – "He brings them out of darknesses into the light." We are not only discussing physical darkness but spiritual darkness as well.

You may find yourself in dark environments, drowned in bad company, surrounded by an unsupportive family where you are the only Muslim or the only Muslim trying to hold onto some semblance of Islam. Your workplace may be terrible – you need the job but everyone around you is constantly engaged in sin: foul language, drinking, immoral behavior. You have no other employment option. You are in a ghasiq – a situation where darkness is pouring out around you. You need Allah ﷻ to tear you out of that, to split that darkness open.

According to Ibn Abbas (RA) and mentioned in hadith literature, some scholars understood ghasiq to refer specifically to the moon as it disappears. Hassan Al-Basri (RA) noted in his dictionary that if we accept this interpretation linguistically, the ghasiq could refer to the moon as it vanishes into the darkness of night – the last remnant of light at night disappearing. The idea being expressed is: I seek Allah's refuge even when that final bit of light is gone, when I see no hope anywhere, when I only see darkness all around me, when even the moon that would tear through the darkness has vanished.

This is the moment when you must remember that Allah ﷻ is رَبِّ الْفَلَقِ – the One who tears all darkness open. No matter how deep the darkness has settled, no matter how completely the evil has poured out, no matter how impossible escape seems, He can split it apart. This is why you had to go to Him. No one else can tear open what He can tear open. You will find yourself in situations where you believe it is absolutely impossible to escape – you have thought about it endlessly and see no plan, no scheme, no amount of money, no help from anyone that can solve this problem. In those situations, understand: no creation can help you. Only Allah ﷻ can make a way that nobody else can see. He alone can tear open darkness like nothing else can.

translation: And from the evil of the blowers in knots
commentary:
وَمِن شَرِّ ٱلنَّفَّـٰثَـٰتِ فِى ٱلْعُقَدِ

The second specific example Allah ﷻ mentions has been the source of much controversy: النَّفَّاثَاتِ فِي الْعُقَدِ – those who blow into knots. The word نَفَّاثَات comes from نَفْث, which means blowing with a bit of spit or saliva, like blowing out a candle. The pattern فَعَّال indicates someone who does this action repeatedly or professionally.

The feminine plural form نَفَّاثَات is used, which in Arabic can refer specifically to women or can be a general plural for people engaged in this practice, since feminine adjectives can apply to masculine nouns as well. Some translations specify "the women who blow on knots," but linguistically it could refer to anyone who engages in this practice. In many historical cultures, witchcraft and occult practices were indeed associated with women, often because women in those societies were disempowered and sought alternative means of influence or control.

This leads us to address a significant matter regarding the context of revelation. There exists a lengthy narration suggesting that a Jewish man named Lubaid, who knew magic, cast a spell on the Prophet ﷺ during the Madinah period. According to this narration, the Prophet ﷺ began forgetting things, contradicting himself, and suffering from intense fever. Angel Jibreel (AS) allegedly informed him in a dream that someone had performed magic on him, that a knotted string with spells blown into it had been thrown in a well, and that he needed to retrieve it and recite these surahs to break the spell.

I must be forthright: I previously accepted this narration years ago when teaching this surah, but my study and understanding have changed significantly since then. Several profound problems emerge with this narrative:

First, from the textual evidence of the Quran itself. The Prophet ﷺ had the strongest heart that ever existed. The Quran that would make a mountain collapse from fear of Allah ﷻ came upon his heart. No human being has ever been as pure or as dignified by Allah ﷻ as His Messenger ﷺ. At the time of Musa (AS), magicians brought great magic, yet Musa (AS) was not affected – he overcame their magic. How could some person blow on knots and affect the heart that contains the Quran like no other heart ever had? If that could happen, where does that leave us?

The Quran states: وَقُلْ جَاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْبَاطِلُ – "Truth has come and falsehood has perished." When truth is in your heart, falsehood disappears. From a mutashabih (textual) perspective, accepting this narration creates serious theological problems. It puts into question the credibility of the one who narrates on behalf of Allah ﷻ. If for six months – as some narrations claim – the Prophet ﷺ was saying things and then denying he said them, this challenges the entire concept of Risalah (prophethood). How can the most authentic narrator ever, the one who narrates directly from Allah ﷻ, have his testimony be unreliable?

Second, the Prophet ﷺ was commanded early in Makkah: وَاصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَاهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا – "Be patient over what they say and leave them in a beautiful manner." Would he then curse someone?

I raised these questions with Dr. Akram Nadwi, a world-renowned hadith scholar who has literally studied hundreds of thousands of narrations and whom scholars worldwide consult for difficult questions. Dr. Nadwi's research confirmed that this narration has serious authenticity issues. The great Imam Al-Jassas in the third century stated emphatically that this was inserted into Islam by those who wanted to destroy it, by the zanadiqa (heretics). This was a serious scholarly debate at one point in Islamic history, but over time one view became more commonly accepted and distributed in tafsir literature.

When you study thousands of narrations about the Prophet ﷺ and the entire Quran, it is like studying a river – the river flows in one direction. Then you find one narration trying to go upstream, one drop trying to go against the entire current. When everything else you have learned flows one way and one narration attempts to flow the opposite direction, something must be questioned. This does not mean we reject all hadith or all narrations, but there must be research done to understand why this one element does not fit with everything else. We cannot do intellectual acrobatics to force it to fit.

What actually happened as a result of accepting this narration? We began believing someone has done sihr (magic) on us, someone has done the evil eye on us, and now we need someone to undo the magic, to recite Quran and blow on us. We took this so far that if an engagement breaks off, we say someone did magic. If you get food poisoning two days after a wedding, we say someone looked at you with the evil eye at the wedding – not that you ate from an unhygienic street vendor.

The evil eye is mentioned in authentic hadith, but how do we understand it? The hadith states that al-'ayn haqq – the eye is real. But 'ayn in that hadith means when someone looks at you with evil intention – that is a real danger. Be mindful of people's facial expressions when someone is looking at you with jealousy, with hatred, with intent to harm. That is real. Sometimes hasad (which we will discuss) lives inside the heart, but if you develop awareness, you can see it in how people look at you. The Quran mentions this: وَإِن يَكَادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَارِهِمْ – "Indeed, those who disbelieve would almost make you slip with their eyes." It almost feels like their stare could knock you down, but the Quran says "almost" – not that it actually will.

We accepted these traditions perhaps because of historical dynamics. When Islam encountered Greek philosophy and rationalism, some groups like the Mu'tazilah wanted to rationalize everything about Islam. They said anything not purely rational should be rejected. They began criticizing traditional scholars: "You people believe in magic and supernatural phenomena – this is not common sense." This created two opposing camps: traditionalists and those influenced by Greek logic.

Here is the problem, illustrated with a modern example: Imagine a Sunni and a Shia having a debate. Unless you happen to be Sunni, and then a Shia says something actually correct – they say something you did not know or did not accept – what is the cultural tendency? "I cannot accept anything he says, because if I do, people will think I've become Shia. I must reject everything that comes from their mouth to prove my Sunni identity." The same happens from the other side. The same dynamics occur between different religions or different schools of thought within Islam.

There was massive Mu'tazili propaganda, creating pressure on the Muslim Ummah: we cannot accept any part of what they say, because accepting any of it means people will assume we are like them. In this environment, perhaps the magic narration became accepted as a way of differentiating from the Mu'tazilah, even though earlier scholars like Al-Jassas and others had serious reservations.

So what does this ayah actually mean? Allah ﷻ is describing a cultural practice that existed: people would tie knots, blow into them with incantations, and claim this would harm someone. This was their weird practice – and it is weird. Does blowing on yarn knots seem normal or logical? This defies common sense. It is insanity. But when people are in desperate situations, they do irrational things. When they abandon reliance on Allah ﷻ, evil emerges – they get taken advantage of, a culture of magic and potions and chants develops.

The same phenomenon exists today in different forms. Maybe not blowing on knots, but going to someone who claims to receive news from the unseen, from jinn, who will tell you that your husband does not talk to you because of some supernatural reason, that you need to move here or quit that job or arrange this marriage based on their supposed mystical knowledge. These charlatans make money by taking advantage of people's naivety. There are Islamic television channels in the Muslim world where people call in asking about personal problems, and the host claims to be "connecting to information" that will solve their issues. In Malaysia, chocolates are sold with labels stating how many times Quran was recited over them, with prices varying based on the number of recitations – as if eating "blessed chocolate" gives you superpowers.

This is what we have become. We have taken the deen of Allah ﷻ and reduced it to the level of nonsensical superstition. When this happens, people are selling you shirk in the name of Islam. This is exactly what the angels Harut and Marut told people when they came seeking magic: إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ – "We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve." Believe in the One who can solve all problems. Why are you turning to this stuff to solve your problems? You can only turn to such things if you have forgotten the One you seek refuge in.

Notice carefully: Allah ﷻ says مِن شَرِّ"from the evil of" – not simply "from those who blow." It is مِن شَرِّ النَّفَّاثَاتِ, just as it says مِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ and later مِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ. Why this pattern? Because these things in themselves are not what you need protection from. You need protection from their evil potential, from the harm they can cause. You do not need to be afraid of the night itself, but of the evil that might emerge in it. You do not need to fear some person blowing on knots, but if you become influenced by such practices – if you accept their power – then their evil will harm you.

The Quran came to make us thinking people: أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ – "Do you not think?" أَفَلَا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ – "Do you not reflect?" Allah ﷻ made us people with eyes open. Do not believe in nonsensical superstition. Save yourselves from it. Save your families from it.

When the Prophet ﷺ himself became seriously ill, what did he do? Anas (RA) narrates: "I became sick. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ came to visit me." What surah was the Prophet ﷺ going to recite to heal the illness? What was he going to do? "He came in, put his hand on my chest, noticed my chest was cold, and said, 'You have a heart problem. You are not doing well.'" Who diagnosed him? The Prophet ﷺ. Then what? "He said, 'Go bring Harith ibn Kaladah from Taif, because he is skilled at healing practices.'" Go bring a doctor. And who was Harith? He was Christian – not even Muslim – but he was a good doctor.

When you have a problem, go to a doctor. The Quran has its own healing power, but we misuse it. The Quran's healing is not: you have foot problems, so recite Quran over your feet. The Quran clearly states where its healing lies: فِيهِ شِفَاءٌ لِّمَا فِي الصُّدُورِ – "In it is healing for what is in the chests." When your iman in Allah ﷻ weakens, when you hear Quran and are reminded of Allah ﷻ, reminded of His power, reminded that only He heals and only He has control – when that iman returns to your heart, then Allah ﷻ blesses you with healing, removes your difficulty, responds to your situation when you truly let go and accept that He is in control.

The words of Allah ﷻ did not come so you hear sounds and blow them on someone. They came لِيَدَّبَّرُوا آيَاتِهِ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ – "So they may reflect upon its ayat and so people of understanding may take remembrance." When Quran is recited to you, what is your obligation as a believer? That your iman increases, that you place more and more trust in Allah ﷻ. These are not magical sounds. Other traditions might recite spells thinking the sounds will heal – that is not our Islam. The Quran came so you benefit from its message, and when you internalize its message, it heals you. That is the healing.

We have done to the Quran what some Jews did to the Torah – we turned it into prescriptions: recite this five times, recite that twenty-seven times, write Quranic letters on paper and put them on the refrigerator. This is silliness. This is not our deen. No Sahabi (RA) did this. This is a disservice to Allah's Book.

The Prophet ﷺ himself is teaching us: the way you get protection is not through superstitious rituals. Imagine it is raining and there is only one building open. You say out loud, "I am going to find shade in this building," but you keep standing outside saying it repeatedly while getting drenched. Are you insane? When you declare you will find shade in the building, it means you actually go inside. When you say أَعُوذُ – "I seek refuge" – you are not just supposed to say those words. You are supposed to actually come under Allah's refuge. How are you humbling yourself? How are you turning to Allah ﷻ to remove this problem?

We have taken the words of Allah ﷻ and made them come from here (the tongue) when they are supposed to come from here (the heart). People blow on knots – their cultural practice of supposed magic. The Quran mentions it not because it has real power, but because when people turn away from Allah ﷻ, they fall into such practices. Today it might not be literal knot-blowing, but there are other forms: Ouija boards, palm reading, people claiming spirits communicate through them, "Islamic" TV shows where supposed mystics give life advice based on claims of unseen knowledge. Families are destroyed because of this. People make terrible life decisions – whom to marry, where to move, which job to quit – based on charlatans who only want money.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from that kind of ignorance. The Quran came to make us people who think.

translation: And from the evil of an envier when he envies
commentary:
وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ

The final evil Allah ﷻ mentions is often translated as "the evil of an envier when he envies" or "the jealous one when he is jealous." But this translation is inadequate. Jealousy is only one small dimension of حَسَد. When we reduce hasad to mere jealousy, we do a profound disservice to understanding what Allah ﷻ is warning us about. As long as you think you do not have a jealousy problem, you might believe you have no hasad problem. But hasad is much bigger, and you might have some other dimension of it that has nothing to do with jealousy.

What is hasad? The scholars define it as a feeling trapped inside the heart of a person because of which they want good things to be taken away from someone else – whether the hasid gets those things or not is irrelevant. The core of hasad is: I do not even want it for myself; I just do not want them to have it. I do not care if I do not get it. I do not even need it. I have a car, but I just hope her car breaks down. I do not even like her car, but I cannot see her happy. I want them to lose what they have, or if they do not have something good yet, I hope they never get it. Whether they get it or lose it adds nothing to you and takes nothing from you, but you are obsessed with depriving someone else.

This can happen because of jealousy – you are jealous because they got attention at school, everyone thinks she is prettier, everyone thinks your brother is smarter. You hope he fails his next class, even though him failing will not make you more successful. That is jealousy-driven hasad.

But sometimes hasad exists when you have nothing to prove, no competition with the person. You are established, respected, at the top of your field. There is someone far below you asking for your advice, and you deliberately give them bad advice: "No, not him. He does not deserve success. I will tell him to go to the worst possible school. I will tell him accounting is better for him than medical school." You throw them off track not because you feel threatened, not because it takes from your status, but because some people find joy in depriving others. They become sadistic. When people become that sick, there is no cure. You cannot change them.

If they are after you like that, you start wondering: Why are they like this? What can I do to change their mind? Why do they hate me so much? They gain nothing from seeing me in pain, yet they seem so happy when I am suffering. What did I ever do to them? I do not even know them well. Why? Allah ﷻ is telling us: when you face someone with hasad, the only One who can protect you is Allah ﷻ. There is no cure mentioned, because if there were, He would have stated it. Diseases of the heart typically have cures: greed has a cure, love of dunya has a cure, desire and temptation have cures. But hasad? When hasad takes over someone's heart, the only thing you can do is ask Allah ﷻ to protect you, to be the barrier between you and them.

The word hasad comes from the root ح س د. Originally, it described ticks – the insects that bite severely and suck blood. The creature that lives off causing pain, that only feels fulfilled when it is hurting something else. That is a hasid. This feeling lives inside a person, and they feed off others' suffering.

The Quran addresses hasad in multiple contexts, showing us its different manifestations. The first and greatest sin began with hasad: Iblis refused to prostrate to Adam (AS), saying essentially, "Why is he being recognized? I should have been recognized." That is where all evil started. The first crime between human beings was driven by hasad – Cain killed Abel. What did Cain gain? Nothing. He did not benefit in any way. At its core, he just could not bear to see his brother succeed.

Sometimes hasad comes from those you would least expect it from. The People of the Book knew scripture more than anyone at their time, yet Allah ﷻ says: مَا يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَلَا الْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ حَسَدًا مِّنْ عِنْدِ أَنْفُسِهِمْ – "Neither those who disbelieve from the People of the Book nor the polytheists wish that any good be sent down to you from your Lord, out of hasad from themselves." They cannot accept you. There is jealousy inside them. They just want to see you hurt.

Sometimes you have disagreements with your own brother, your friend, your cousin, your parents. You are right, and they know you are right. But if they accept that you are right, you will feel good about yourself, and they cannot let that happen. So they will make you feel terrible. Later they might admit to someone else, "Yes, that was true, but why would I let them feel good?" This is hasad – being obsessed with not letting someone feel relief, being consumed by someone else's pain.

The Quran describes this psychology powerfully: إِن تَمْسَسْكُمْ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِن تُصِبْكُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَفْرَحُوا بِهَا – "If good touches you, it distresses them; but if disaster strikes you, they rejoice at it." If something good happens to you, they are in a horrible mood. Nothing bad happened to them – roof over their head, food on the plate, everything fine – but they are disgusted, deeply upset because they found out you graduated, you got a job, you received some blessing. And if something terrible happens to you, suddenly they are in a wonderful mood, overwhelmed with joy.

You might be thinking of someone right now. I do not know who, but you know. Listen to what Allah ﷻ says at the end of that ayah: وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئًا – "But if you are patient and conscious of Allah, their plotting will not harm you at all." Then He adds: إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ – "Indeed, Allah is Encompassing of what they do."

For a long time, I did not fully understand the ending of this ayah. Allah ﷻ says when something bad happens to you they rejoice, when something good happens to you they are distressed, and then He addresses you: if you can have sabr (patience) and taqwa (consciousness of Allah), that is one of the greatest accomplishments. Why would Allah ﷻ need to emphasize that having sabr and taqwa in this situation is such an incredible achievement? It must mean that when this happens to you, it will severely test your sabr and your taqwa.

I consulted with counselors about this, and received remarkable insights. When people do horrible things to you, when people find joy in your misery, you become constantly worried: What are they going to do next? How will they hurt me next? You become paranoid about their next criticism, their next attack. Sometimes they do not even have to do anything physical – just the expression on their face as they see you in pain tears you apart. Every time something good happens to you, you think: They are going to be really upset about this. How are they going to ruin this for me?

You become obsessed with those who have hasad toward you. Instead of finding your strength in Allah ﷻ, instead of persevering and saying, "This is nothing – Allah ﷻ protects me from this, these people can do nothing, I do not have to worry about them," you are constantly worried. If I do this, what will they say? They already said this and that. How will they spin this against me? What will they say to people? How will they try to destroy me? You become paranoid, consumed with thoughts of them.

You might protest: "I am not paranoid – they have done it before! They have shown their hasad!" Allah ﷻ acknowledges: قَدْ بَدَتِ الْبَغْضَاءُ مِنْ أَفْوَاهِهِمْ وَمَا تُخْفِي صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ – "Hatred has already appeared from their mouths, and what their chests conceal is greater." Yes, their animosity is real. But when it consumes you, when you are on edge all the time thinking about them, you no longer have sabr. Sabr is a source of strength. When you lose sabr, you lose taqwa as well.

Taqwa comes from وَقَى, which means to protect, to seek protection. Having taqwa means seeking Allah's protection, truly believing at that moment: The only One who can protect me is Allah ﷻ. So long as I am mindful of Him, fearful of Him, protecting myself by seeking His refuge and ensuring I do not make Him angry, then nobody else's anger, nobody else's evil intent, nobody else's harm can do anything to me. When you reach that state – when you can overcome and blind yourself to all their scheming, when you remain patient and conscious of Allah ﷻ despite being the target of hasad – that is an amazing, rare accomplishment.

But it becomes even harder because sometimes you are the only one who sees it. You might be a victim of hasad, genuinely targeted by people who want to see you harmed for no logical reason – they just hate you, they do not like your face, that is enough for them. You might talk to your mother, your brother, your spouse about it, and they say, "No big deal. Why are you so worried about that?" They do not see it. You see it. Allah ﷻ is telling us: nobody else can see what is in their hearts except Allah ﷻ.

Sometimes the pain someone causes is not physical. If somebody cuts you, you can show the wound. But the pain someone causes with the way they look at you? You go to a gathering and they look at you like you are nothing. You come back and say, "Did you notice how they were looking at me?" And the response: "What? They were just looking. They were just eating food. You are being paranoid." No, you noticed it. People's facial expressions, their intonations, the way they speak – all of this matters. A frown matters. A sneer matters. The Quran mentions this: وَإِذَا رَأَوْكَ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا – when they see you, they take you in mockery. Facial expressions matter. Allah ﷻ even highlights arrogance through facial expressions.

When you notice their hasad and nobody else does, Allah ﷻ is telling you: I see it. I know. Because hasad lives in here (the heart), and very little comes out. But what if it does come out? This is why the ayah says: مِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ – "from the evil of the envier when he envies/when he acts on his envy." Allah ﷻ knows it is inside them. You seek His protection from the evil potential they have, from what might emerge when they act on that hatred.

There is a hadith about a different dimension of hasad. The Prophet ﷺ said: "There is no admirable envy except in two cases." He uses the word hasad, but means ghubta (admiring someone and wishing for similar blessings for yourself, without wanting theirs taken away): "A person whom Allah ﷻ has given Quran to, and he stands with that Quran in the middle of the night and throughout the day – that is someone you should wish to be like. And another person whom Allah ﷻ has given wealth to, and he is charitable day and night, constantly helping others – you could wish to be like him." You could have much money and show it off, or you could have much money and constantly give it away. These are people worth emulating.

But listen to this warning: "The diseases that came to previous nations will come to my Ummah." The Companions (RA) asked, "Messenger of Allah ﷺ, what are these deadly diseases?" He ﷺ listed: First, ingratitude (shuh) – you will have blessings but only think about your problems, never about the good you have. You will always complain. Second, arrogance and showing off (kibr). Third, competing with each other (tanafus) – constantly comparing yourself to others: he has more, I want more than him. Fourth, grinding yourself to acquire more in this world (haras) – literally killing yourself: working 100 hours a week, 120 hours a week, destroying your health, your sleep, your relationships, but "I need this money, I need to get somewhere." Fifth, hatred for one another (baghda) – when you compete, you start thinking the only way to get ahead is by cutting others down. You become cutthroats. This exists in corporate culture: people smile at your face while working behind the scenes to get you fired. The Prophet ﷺ warned this would not just happen in business – it would happen in matters of deen. "One day I will have more YouTube views than you." "One day I will be the keynote speaker instead of you." It is pathetic. The battle to be recognized, the mutual hatred.

When I first entered Islamic circles, coming from a different background, I was shocked. I was in gatherings of people I admired for their knowledge, their scholarship, yet in those very circles I heard them speaking with hatred about scholars from other institutions, other schools of thought, other masajid. It was the ugliest experience. I have been at conferences where people gave me rides and during the drive casually said, "The shaykh who organized this is a good guy – I mean, he steals money from orphans, but he is good otherwise." I was stunned. Are you going to eat chicken after consuming his flesh? Seriously? Even if what you said were true, should you say it? This is not how we should behave. Finding something to tear someone else down, not because you gain anything from it – that is hasad.

How much of our culture now, with social media and YouTube – Alhamdulillah there are many opportunities for dawah – but how many scholars are obsessed with ripping someone else apart? All they want to talk about is how wrong this person is, how he is going to Hell. Is that all that is left of your deen? The Quran is not interesting enough? There is no Surah Al-Baqarah to teach? No reminders to share? Why are you so obsessed with someone else? Why do you care if people are still listening to him? What does it do to you?

People used to approach me constantly: "This person is deviant. That person has bid'ah. Don't speak at this conference because that shaykh is attending and he is clearly misguided. If you share a stage with him, you support his misguidance." I would respond: I actually like him. I disagree with him on some things, but I still like him, and he is really good at what he does. "You are going to share a stage with a deviant?" I would say: I have shared stages with Christians, Jews, atheists. Does it matter? If he is saying something false, why don't you say what is true? If what you say is true, then وَقُلْ جَاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْبَاطِلُ – truth comes forward and falsehood disappears. Instead of obsessing over "that is false, that is false," why not just teach the truth? If your truth is powerful enough, it will replace the false. Do not be obsessed with people. That is hasad.

The Prophet ﷺ concluded: "And these diseases will spread until rebellion against Allah ﷻ emerges" – meaning from that hasad, you will do the worst things against each other. In another narration, he ﷺ said: "The sickness of previous nations will come to you: jealousy and hatred (hasad and baghda)." Then he said something profound: "This hatred – it shaves." He clarified: "I do not mean it shaves hair. I mean it shaves your religion away." 

Why did he ﷺ not just say it destroys religion? Because when you shave hair, it grows back. This is the eloquence of the Prophet ﷺ: hasad shaves away your deen, but it can return. Get rid of the hatred from your life, and your iman can grow back. But while it is there, it consumes your religion.

Hasad happens in worldly matters – wanting someone to lose money, house, fame, material things. But there is another brand of hasad related to religion, and that is the scariest. People who serve Allah ﷻ and work for the Ummah – the very few people with knowledge of Islam – unfortunately often have fights among themselves. When they have conflicts and want to see each other go down, when they are busy with this, what happens to the rest of the Ummah? Nobody cares for the masses anymore. People only care if someone shows up at "their" masjid instead of "that" masjid: "Why did you come here? Don't you know the mistakes of these people?" It becomes about turf, market share, slicing up the deen. You become Shia and factions that fight and hate each other. This is actually a punishment from Allah ﷻ on an Ummah.

The evil of حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ – when hasad is acted upon – spreads suffering to people not even involved. The harm spreads and spreads and spreads. May Allah ﷻ protect us from becoming people of hasad, and may He protect us from people of hasad.

Notice: hasad does not necessarily mean they act on it. It means they carry it inside them. That is the most dangerous – when you do not know what is going on inside somebody. Remove it from your hearts. If you have been obsessed with thoughts of hurting somebody, seeing somebody fail, let it go. If they have done something wrong, Allah ﷻ will deal with them – not you. Be grateful Allah ﷻ did not make you the judge, or me the judge, for dispensing justice. If human beings were given that right, we would all be dead. We would kill each other. It would be finished.

When you find yourself targeted by hasad, remember these profound words: وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا – "If you are patient and conscious of Allah" – لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئًا – "their plotting will not harm you at all." Turn to the Master of Al-Falaq, the One who tears open every darkness, every difficulty, every impossible situation. Cling to Him, make Him your refuge, and understand that He is telling you to seek His protection not because these things have ultimate power, but because their evil potential is real – and only He can shield you from it.

This is the essence of Surah Al-Falaq: recognizing the reality of external threats while understanding that the One you turn to is infinitely greater than all of them combined. Say it, mean it, live it: قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ.
